step.title: Step 13
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> DOWN -> DOWN

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 34 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 241, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT': 'Move player 3 pixels in that direction between adjacent 3x3 cells'}
- Object taxonomy: {'b': 'blue player (3x3 block, 8 pixels)', 'N': 'light green target (3x3 block)', 'W': 'white cell (3x3 block)', 'g': 'gray cell (3x3 block)', 'B': 'black wall/background', 'M': 'pink HUD bar (bottom row)', 'c': 'charcoal single-pixel object at (23,40)'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_row': 'pink M-colored HUD/timer bar'}
- Win pattern: Reach the green N cell to complete the level
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 240 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 12.6x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 153 actions returned the board to a state already seen 153 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 153 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The path execution didn't cause game over, but it also didn't complete the level. The player moved 34 steps but didn't reach the target. Let me check where the player is now.
Looking at the image, the player (blue) appears to be somewhere in the middle-right area, around row 19-22, column 34-37.
Let me check the current player position and figure out what went wrong with the path.
The path was:
[(16, 22), (19, 22), (22, 22), (25, 22), (28, 22), (28, 25), (28, 28), (25, 28), (22, 28), (22, 31), (22, 34), (25, 34), (28, 34), (31, 34), (34, 34), (34, 31), (34, 28), (34, 25), (34, 22), (37, 22), (40, 22), (40, 25), (40, 28), (40, 31), (40, 34), (43, 34), (46, 34), (46, 37), (46, 40), (43, 40), (40, 40), (40, 43), (40, 46), (43, 46), (46, 46)]
But the player didn't end at (46,46). Looking at the image, the player is at approximately (19,34) or (22,34).
Wait, let me re-examine the image. The player (blue) is in the upper right area. Looking at the grid:
- Row 16: W g b W g W g W (player was at 16,22)
- After DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN: player should be at (28,22)
- After RIGHT, RIGHT: player should be at (28,28)
- After UP, UP: player should be at (22,28)
- After RIGHT, RIGHT: player should be at (22,34)
...
But the image shows the player at approximately (19,34). Let me check the segmentation to find the exact position.
Actually, I think the issue is that the path I computed was wrong. The BFS found a path, but the player didn't follow it correctly. This could be because:
1. The cell graph changed between frames
2. The player moved differently than expected
3. The path was blocked at some point
Let me check the current player position and then recompute the path from there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current player position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        pr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        pc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"Player: ({pr},{pc})")
        break

# Check target position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        tr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        tc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"Target: ({tr},{tc})")
        break

# Build cell graph again
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
cells = {}
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('W', 'g', 'N', 'b'):
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        r = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        c = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        cells[(r,c)] = n['color']

# Build adjacency
adj = {}
node_list = list(cells.keys())
for p1 in node_list:
    adj[p1] = []
    for p2 in node_list:
        if p1 == p2: continue
        r1, c1 = p1
        r2, c2 = p2
        dist = abs(r1-r2) + abs(c1-c2)
        if dist == 3:
            adj[p1].append(p2)

# BFS from player to target
from collections import deque
player = None
target = None
for pos, color in cells.items():
    if color == 'b': player = pos
    if color == 'N': target = pos

print(f"\nPlayer: {player}, Target: {target}")

queue = deque([(player, [player])])
visited = {player}
found_path = None

while queue:
    pos, path = queue.popleft()
    if pos == target:
        found_path = path
        break
    for nb in adj.get(pos, []):
        if nb not in visited:
            visited.add(nb)
            queue.append((nb, path + [nb]))

if found_path:
    print(f"Path length: {len(found_path)-1} steps")
    actions = []
    for i in range(1, len(found_path)):
        r1, c1 = found_path[i-1]
        r2, c2 = found_path[i]
        if r2 > r1: actions.append('DOWN')
        elif r2 < r1: actions.append('UP')
        elif c2 > c1: actions.append('RIGHT')
        elif c2 < c1: actions.append('LEFT')
    print(f"Actions: {actions}")
    result = action(actions)
    print(f"Result: game_over={result.get('game_over')}, done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")
    
    # Check final position
    for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b':
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            pr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
            pc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
            print(f"Final player: ({pr},{pc})")
            break
else:
    print("No path found!")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Move player 3 pixels in that direction"}, "object_taxonomy": {"b": "blue player", "N": "light green target", "W": "white cell", "g": "gray cell", "B": "black wall", "M": "pink HUD"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_row": "pink HUD"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player moved 34 steps but did not reach target. Need to check actual position and recompute path.", "goal_model": "Navigate from player to (46,46) target.", "recent_findings": ["34-step path did not cause game over", "Player position may not match expected path"], "open_questions": ["Where is player actually? Why did path not work?"], "current_plan": "Find player position, BFS to target, execute.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current player position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        pr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        pc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"Player: ({pr},{pc})")
        break

# Check target position
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        tr = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        tc = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        print(f"Target: ({tr},{tc})")
        break

# Build cell graph again
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
cells = {}
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('W', 'g', 'N', 'b'):
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        r = (min(rows)+max(rows))//2
        c = (min(cols)+max(cols))//2
        cells[(r,c)] = n['color']

# Build adjacency
adj = {}
node_list = list(cells.keys())
for p1 in node_list:
    adj[p1] = []
    for p2 in node_list:
        if p1 == p2: continue
        r1, c1 = p1
        r2, c2 = p2
        dist = abs(r1-r2) + abs(c1-c2)
        if dist == 3:
            adj[p1].append(p2)

# BFS from player to target
from collections import deque
player = None
target = None
for pos, color in cells.items():
    if color == 'b': player = pos
    if color == 'N': target = pos

print(f"\nPlayer: {player}, Target: {target}")

queue = deque([(player, [player])])
visited = {player}
found_path = None

while queue:
    pos, path = queue.popleft()
    if pos == target:
        found_path = path
        break
    for nb in adj.get(pos, []):

            visited.add(nb)
            queue.append((nb, path + [nb]))

if found_path:

            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
